title: RED TAPE Act
stage_label: Committee on Small Business and Entrepreneurship. Hearings held.
plain_summary: The RED TAPE Act says that when government agencies make new rules, they can only count benefits that have a clear dollar amount. Agencies cannot use benefits that are hard to put a price on, like cleaner air or safer neighborhoods. If an agency uses these non-dollar benefits anyway, courts must throw out their rule completely. The bill also requires agencies to publish their cost and benefit studies for everyone to see. This law would start working 30 days after Congress passes it.
last_action_text: Committee on Small Business and Entrepreneurship. Hearings held.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Regulations Evaluated to Determine The Anticipated Price and Effect Act or the RED TAPE Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress believes that government agencies must focus on clear, measurable money benefits when they make decisions. This means that when agencies create new rules, those rules should provide obvious financial benefits to both the public and private businesses. At the same time, agencies should keep unnecessary costs and burdens as low as possible.

sections.title: Section 3  Prohibition on Net Benefit Discounts; Consideration of Regulatory Impact Analyses
sections.text: This section changes the law to add new definitions and rules. It defines benefit-cost analysis as the method described in a government document called Office of Management and Budget Circular A-94 from November 9, 2023. It also defines regulatory impact analysis as the type of study described in several presidential orders and government circulars that guide how agencies review their rules. The section creates a new rule that says agencies cannot consider any benefits or factors that cannot be measured in dollars when they study the impact of new rules. The Office of Management and Budget also cannot allow or encourage agencies to use these unmeasurable factors. Agencies must publish detailed information about their studies in the Federal Register, including their methods and reasoning. Within ninety days of this law taking effect, the Office of Management and Budget must give agencies new guidance to follow these rules. If an agency breaks these rules by considering unmeasurable factors, anyone affected by the rule can sue the agency in federal court. If the court finds the agency did consider unmeasurable factors, the court must declare the rule invalid. This applies to any rule made on or after November 9, 2023.

sections.title: Section 4  Effective Date
sections.text: The changes made by Section 3 will start working thirty days after this law is signed.
